channel.title: Domain-Error on ErrorCode — Error Code Reference & Solutions
channel.description: Recent content in Domain-Error on ErrorCode — Error Code Reference & Solutions

item.title: [Solution] C++ std::domain_error - acos domain error
item.description:
std::domain_error - acos domain error

std::domain_error is thrown when std::acos receives an argument outside [-1, 1]. Due to floating-point precision, values like 1.0000000001 can appear.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Argument > 1
double result = std::acos(1.1); // domain error

// Cause 2: Argument < -1
double result = std::acos(-1.1); // domain error

// Cause 3: Floating-point precision
double result = std::acos(std::cos(M_PI)); // may be slightly > 1

How to Fix

Fix 1: Clamp argument

double safe_acos(double x) {
 x = std::max(-1.0, std::min(1.0, x));
 return std::acos(x);
}

Fix 2: Check before calling

if (x >= -1.0 && x <= 1.0) {
 double angle = std::acos(x);
}

Fix 3: Use asin for better range

double angle = std::asin(std::sqrt(1 - x * x));

Related Errors

  • std::domain_error - asin — asin domain error.
  • std::domain_error - sqrt — sqrt of negative.
  • std::domain_error - log — log domain error.

item.title: [Solution] C++ std::domain_error - asin domain error
item.description:
std::domain_error - asin domain error

std::domain_error is thrown when std::asin receives an argument outside [-1, 1].

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Argument > 1
double result = std::asin(1.1); // domain error

// Cause 2: Argument < -1
double result = std::asin(-1.1); // domain error

// Cause 3: Precision issue
double x = 1.0000000001;
double result = std::asin(x); // may throw

How to Fix

Fix 1: Clamp argument

double safe_asin(double x) {
 x = std::max(-1.0, std::min(1.0, x));
 return std::asin(x);
}

Fix 2: Use atan2 for robust angle calculation

double angle = std::atan2(y, x); // always safe

Fix 3: Check before calling

if (std::abs(x) <= 1.0) {
 double angle = std::asin(x);
}

Related Errors

  • std::domain_error - acos — acos domain error.
  • std::domain_error - sqrt — sqrt of negative.
  • std::domain_error - log — log domain error.

item.title: [Solution] C++ std::domain_error - log of zero/negative
item.description:
std::domain_error - log of zero/negative

std::domain_error is thrown when std::log or std::log2 or std::log10 receives zero or a negative argument.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: log of zero
double result = std::log(0.0); // -infinity, may throw

// Cause 2: log of negative
double result = std::log(-1.0); // domain error

// Cause 3: log2 of zero
double result = std::log2(0.0); // -infinity

How to Fix

Fix 1: Validate input

double safe_log(double x) {
 if (x <= 0) throw std::domain_error("log of non-positive");
 return std::log(x);
}

Fix 2: Check before calling

if (x > 0) {
 double result = std::log(x);
}

Fix 3: Use log1p for small values

double safe_log1p(double x) {
 if (x <= -1) throw std::domain_error("log1p argument <= -1");
 return std::log1p(x);
}

Related Errors

  • std::domain_error - sqrt — sqrt of negative.
  • std::domain_error - acos — acos out of range.
  • std::domain_error - asin — asin out of range.

item.title: [Solution] C++ std::domain_error - math domain error
item.description:
std::domain_error - math domain error

std::domain_error is thrown when a math function receives an argument outside its valid mathematical domain.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: sqrt of negative
double result = std::sqrt(-1.0); // domain error

// Cause 2: log of zero or negative
double result = std::log(-1.0); // domain error

// Cause 3: acos with value > 1
double result = std::acos(2.0); // domain error

How to Fix

Fix 1: Validate before math

double safe_sqrt(double x) {
 if (x < 0) throw std::domain_error("sqrt of negative");
 return std::sqrt(x);
}

Fix 2: Use complex numbers

#include <complex>
std::complex<double> result = std::sqrt(std::complex<double>(-1, 0));

Fix 3: Clamp values

double safe_acos(double x) {
 x = std::max(-1.0, std::min(1.0, x));
 return std::acos(x);
}

Related Errors

  • std::domain_error - sqrt — sqrt specific.
  • std::domain_error - log — log specific.
  • std::domain_error - acos — acos specific.

item.title: [Solution] C++ std::domain_error - pow function error
item.description:
std::domain_error - pow function error

std::domain_error from std::pow occurs when arguments are mathematically invalid, such as negative base with fractional exponent.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Negative base with fractional exponent
double result = std::pow(-1.0, 0.5); // domain error

// Cause 2: 0^negative
double result = std::pow(0.0, -1.0); // domain error (infinity)

// Cause 3: Complex result
double result = std::pow(-2.0, 1.5); // domain error

How to Fix

Fix 1: Validate before calling

double safe_pow(double base, double exp) {
 if (base < 0 && exp != std::floor(exp)) {
 throw std::domain_error("negative base with fractional exponent");
 }
 return std::pow(base, exp);
}

Fix 2: Use std::abs for negative bases

double result = std::pow(std::abs(-1.0), 0.5);

Fix 3: Use complex pow

#include <complex>
auto result = std::pow(std::complex<double>(-1, 0), 0.5);

Related Errors

  • std::domain_error - sqrt — sqrt of negative.
  • std::domain_error - log — log of zero.
  • std::domain_error - acos — acos out of range.

item.title: [Solution] C++ std::domain_error - sqrt of negative
item.description:
std::domain_error - sqrt of negative

std::domain_error is thrown when std::sqrt receives a negative argument (for real number output).

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Direct negative
double result = std::sqrt(-4.0); // domain error

// Cause 2: Variable becomes negative
double x = get_value(); // may be negative
double root = std::sqrt(x); // throws if x < 0

// Cause 3: Expression producing negative
double result = std::sqrt(a - b); // if a < b

How to Fix

Fix 1: Check before calling

double safe_sqrt(double x) {
 if (x < 0) throw std::domain_error("sqrt of negative");
 return std::sqrt(x);
}

Fix 2: Use std::abs

double result = std::sqrt(std::abs(x)); // always positive

Fix 3: Use complex numbers

#include <complex>
auto result = std::sqrt(std::complex<double>(x, 0));

Related Errors

  • std::domain_error - pow — pow domain error.
  • std::domain_error - log — log domain error.
  • std::domain_error - acos — acos domain error.

item.title: [Solution] C++ std::domain_error — Mathematical Domain Error Fix
item.description:
[Solution] C++ std::domain_error — Mathematical Domain Error Fix

A std::domain_error is thrown when a mathematical function is called with an argument outside its valid domain. For example, taking the square root of a negative number, computing the log of zero, or applying an inverse trigonometric function to a value outside [-1, 1]. It inherits from std::logic_error.

Why std::domain_error Occurs

Common causes include computing sqrt() of a negative number, computing log() or log10() of zero or a negative number, applying asin() or acos() to values outside [-1, 1], and custom mathematical functions receiving invalid inputs.

item.title: [Solution] C++ std::domain_error — Mathematical Domain Error Example
item.description:
[Solution] C++ std::domain_error — Mathematical Domain Error Example

A std::domain_error is thrown when a function receives an argument outside its mathematical domain — meaning the operation is not defined for that input. Common examples include taking the square root of a negative number, computing the logarithm of a non-positive number, or using inverse trigonometric functions outside their valid range.

Common Causes

  • Computing sqrt() of a negative number
  • Computing log() or log10() of zero or negative values
  • Using asin() or acos() with values outside [-1, 1]
  • Performing mathematical operations where the result would be undefined

Example: Throwing std::domain_error

#include <cmath>
#include <iostream>
#include <stdexcept>

double safe_sqrt(double x) {
 if (x < 0.0) {
 throw std::domain_error("sqrt: negative argument not in domain");
 }
 return std::sqrt(x);
}

int main() {
 try {
 double result = safe_sqrt(-1.0);
 std::cout << result << std::endl;
 } catch (const std::domain_error& e) {
 std::cerr << "Domain error: " << e.what() << std::endl;
 return 1;
 }
 return 0;
}

How to Fix: Validate Domain Before Operations

#include <cmath>
#include <iostream>
#include <stdexcept>

double safe_log(double x) {
 if (x <= 0.0) {
 throw std::domain_error("log: argument must be positive");
 }
 return std::log(x);
}

double safe_asin(double x) {
 if (x < -1.0 || x > 1.0) {
 throw std::domain_error("asin: argument must be in [-1, 1]");
 }
 return std::asin(x);
}

int main() {
 try {
 std::cout << "log(1) = " << safe_log(1.0) << std::endl;
 std::cout << "log(-1) = " << safe_log(-1.0) << std::endl;
 } catch (const std::domain_error& e) {
 std::cerr << "Domain error: " << e.what() << std::endl;
 }
 return 0;
}

Mathematical Domain Reference

Function Valid Domain Invalid Input
sqrt(x) x >= 0 x < 0
log(x) x > 0 x <= 0
log10(x) x > 0 x <= 0
asin(x) -1 <= x <= 1 x < -1 or x > 1
acos(x) -1 <= x <= 1 x < -1 or x > 1
pow(x, y) x > 0 or (x = 0, y > 0) x = 0, y <= 0

Summary

Fix When to Use
Validate arguments before math functions Always when inputs come from users
Use safe wrapper functions When multiple calls need validation
Return NaN or fallback for invalid domains When exceptions are too expensive
Document mathematical constraints For public math libraries

Related Errors

  • std::logic_error — logical precondition violations.
  • std::invalid_argument — invalid function arguments.
  • std::overflow_error — arithmetic overflow.

item.title: [Solution] C++ std::domain_error — Mathematical Domain Error Fix
item.description:
[Solution] C++ std::domain_error — Mathematical Domain Error Fix

A std::domain_error is thrown when a function receives an argument that is outside its mathematical domain — meaning the operation is not defined for that input. Common examples include taking the square root of a negative number, computing the logarithm of a non-positive number, or using inverse trigonometric functions outside their valid range.

Why std::domain_error Occurs

Common causes include computing sqrt() of a negative number, computing log() or log10() of zero or negative values, using asin() or acos() with values outside [-1, 1], and performing mathematical operations where the result would be undefined.
